question: What dialect and region should the translation target?

options.label: Mexican Spanish
options.style_guide_effect:
DIALECT AND REGISTER
Translate into standard, educated Mexican Spanish. Use tú or Usted forms (not vos or vosotros). Avoid Iberian-specific vocabulary and expressions, prefer MX-standard lexical choices throughout.

options.label: Castilian Spanish
options.style_guide_effect:
DIALECT AND REGISTER
Translate into standard Castilian Spanish. Use tú/vosotros forms as appropriate. Prefer Iberian vocabulary and idiomatic expressions.

options.label: Rioplatense Spanish
options.style_guide_effect:
DIALECT AND REGISTER
Translate into Rioplatense Spanish. Use vos/ustedes forms. Prefer Argentine/Uruguayan vocabulary and expressions.

options.label: Colombian Spanish
options.style_guide_effect:
DIALECT AND REGISTER
Translate into standard Colombian Spanish. Use usted and tú forms as appropriate regionally. Prefer Colombian vocabulary.

question: How should forms of address (you/your) be handled?

options.label: Tú throughout (informal)
options.style_guide_effect:
FORMS OF ADDRESS
Use tú forms for all second-person address. This creates an informal, intimate tone throughout.

options.label: Usted throughout (formal)
options.style_guide_effect:
FORMS OF ADDRESS
Use usted forms for all second-person address. This maintains formal, respectful distance throughout.

options.label: Mixed: tú for family/friends, usted for strangers
options.style_guide_effect:
FORMS OF ADDRESS
Use tú for characters in close relationships (family, friends, children) and usted for strangers, authority figures, and formal contexts. This reflects natural social register switching.

options.label: Vos (regional informal)
options.style_guide_effect:
FORMS OF ADDRESS
Use vos forms for informal second-person address, matching regional usage. Use usted for formal contexts.

custom_prompt: Describe the forms of address rules:

options.label: Raya (—) — standard Spanish convention
options.style_guide_effect:
DIALOGUE FORMATTING
Mark dialogue using standard Spanish raya conventions (—). Break character speech into a new paragraph when it follows narrative. Use guillemets (« ») for nested speech (dialogue within dialogue).

options.label: Quotation marks (
options.style_guide_effect:
DIALOGUE FORMATTING
Mark dialogue using quotation marks (
). Use single quotes ('...') for nested speech.

options.label: Guillemets («...»)
options.style_guide_effect:
DIALOGUE FORMATTING
Mark dialogue using guillemets («...»). Use quotation marks for nested speech within guillemets.

question: How should personal and place names be handled?

options.label: Keep original names unchanged
options.style_guide_effect:
NAME HANDLING
Keep all personal names and place names in their original form. Do not translate or adapt names.

options.label: Translate names when a standard equivalent exists
options.style_guide_effect:
NAME HANDLING
Translate names when a widely recognized equivalent exists in the target language (e.g., Peter → Pedro, London → Londres). Keep names without standard equivalents in their original form.

options.label: Translate all names
options.style_guide_effect:
NAME HANDLING
Translate or adapt all personal and place names to the target language. Create natural-sounding equivalents where standard translations don't exist.
